For instance, theories like foraging subsistence vs. farming or coastal vs. inland dwelling suggest pressures that might favor specific cognitive traits or social behaviors in isolated groups. Ultimately, most experts emphasize that while some localized adaptation may exist, human behavioral plasticity is far greater than current selection pressures allow, explaining why observed social patterns rarely defy individual agency or external circumstance.
